repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
- Make "name" a required positional param of new() instad of an optional `opts` field.
- Restore missing "should log" feature (used [here](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/5370eb01469622e02c5831b3c4063c02449a4c5c/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/handlers.lua#L703)).
- Restore the "should log?" feature which was accidentally(?) lost in 38838fb, when the
level filter was inlined into `format_func`.

comment_data.body: Make `name` a required, positional param. Is more readable, and usually don't want required fields in `opts` (unless the "kwargs" pattern gains significant readability).
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3868,12 +3868,12 @@ Lua module: vim.log                                                  *vim.log*
 
 The |vim.log| module provides file-backed logger instances.
 
-Use `vim.log.new()` to create a logger, it exposes five writer functions:
-`trace()`, `debug()`, `info()`, `warn()`, and `error()`. They are correspond
-to log levels defined by |vim.log.levels|.
+Use `vim.log.new()` to create a logger, which exposes a writer function for
+each |vim.log.levels| level: `trace()`, `debug()`, `info()`, `warn()`, and
+`error()`.
 
 Example: >lua
-    local log = vim.log.new({ name = 'my-plugin', })
+    local log = vim.log.new('my-plugin')

comment_data.body: @ofseed I didn't understand why `set_format_func` is needed, doesn't this achieve the same? Probably not common anyway...
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3882,14 +3882,15 @@ Example: >lua
 
     -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
     -- since the default level is `WARN`.
-    vim.log.set_level(log, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+    log:set_level(vim.log.levels.INFO)
     log.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
     -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
     --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
 <
 
-You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output
-format.
+To customize the output format, override `log.fmt` directly: >lua
+    log.fmt = function(current_level, level, ...) ... end

comment_data.body:
The "should log" feature is used here:
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/5370eb01469622e02c5831b3c4063c02449a4c5c/runtime/lua/vim/lsp/handlers.lua#L703

idk if we want a `vim.lsp.should_log` instead? Somehow or other we want this feature, so that callers can avoid expensive work.

+--- lets callers cheaply guard expensive message construction:

comment_data.body:
do we ever `:close()` the handle?

also should we `:flush()` less frequently? that will require routing all log calls through the same "sink", which is probably a good idea anyway.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -221,59 +223,59 @@ end
 
 --- Creates a writer function for a specific log level.
 ---
+--- The returned writer supports a "should-log" check: calling it with no
+--- arguments returns `true` if the level is enabled, `false` otherwise. This
+--- lets callers cheaply guard expensive message construction:
+--- ```lua
+--- if log.trace() then
+---   log.trace('heavy', vim.inspect(big_table))
+--- end
+--- ```
+---
 ---@package
 ---@param level vim.log.levels
----@return fun(...:any): boolean? # Returns `false` if the log file could not be opened.
+---@return fun(...:any):boolean?
 function M:create_writer(level)
   return function(...)
+    if level < self.level then
+      return false
+    end
     local argc = select('#', ...)
     if argc == 0 then
       return true
     end
     if not self:open_file() then
       return false
     end
-    local message = self.format_func(self.current_level, level, ...)
+    -- TODO(justinmk): should we use `self:fmt()` here?
+    local message = self.fmt(self.level, level, ...)
     if message then
       assert(self.logfile)
       self.logfile:write(message)
+      -- TODO(justinmk):
+      -- - Do this less often... May require a sharing the file handle.
+      --   All logger(x) instances should route through the same sink(x) instance.
+      -- - When/where is logfile closed?
       self.logfile:flush()

comment_data.body:
The main point is that a getter pattern like `set_format_func` makes it easier for us to know the timing and trigger certain operations.

Regarding the buffered logging we plan to implement (I have a simple attempt locally), we might keep logs in memory and write them only at specific times, and it seems a good time to do so when a user changes the `format_func`. We could track when a user modifies `fmt` via a metatable, but a setter would be more straightforward.

This is similar to `level`; we probably need to keep such a setter so that it's more direct to close the file when the level is set to an exceptionally high value.

comment_data.body:
In Lua, the `opts` in `new(opts)` is often defined as a superclass of the object it creates, because in this case `opts` can be referred to as `self`, allowing for the choice between calling it via `.new(self)` or `:new()`. This facilitates the implementation of inheritance.

I have no intention of using such elaborate OOP, but since we had no prior precedent for providing `new`, I am simply following an established convention. I do not have a strong opinion on this.

comment_data.body:
> do we ever `:close()` the handle?

If we switch to using `uv` instead of `io` for file I/O, we will definitely need a `:close` method; I have a WIP branch for buffer implementation that adds this. If implemented with `uv`, we must call `:close` within `__gc` to avoid memory leaks, whereas the `io` module seems to be managed by Lua itself and thus requires no manual cleanup.

> also should we `:flush()` less frequently?

In my WIP branch, I intend to perform the actual writes when application focus changes (via autocmd), while keeping messages in memory using `string.buffer` in the meantime.

comment_data.body:
> a getter pattern like `set_format_func` makes it easier for us to know the timing and trigger certain operations.

Makes sense, but defining a formatter is usually a create-time decision. Until there is a real use-case I don't think we need to care about this.

> we might keep logs in memory and write them only at specific times, and it seems a good time to do so when a user changes the `format_func`

So in that rare case, what is the worst that might happen?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3882,14 +3882,15 @@ Example: >lua
 
     -- Set the log level to `INFO`, otherwise the `info()` call below would be ignored,
     -- since the default level is `WARN`.
-    vim.log.set_level(log, vim.log.levels.INFO)
+    log:set_level(vim.log.levels.INFO)
     log.info('starting', { buf = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf() })
     -- This will write a line like the following to the log file:
     --   [INFO][2024-01-01 12:01:00] source.lua:124    starting    { buf = 1 }
 <
 
-You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output
-format.
+To customize the output format, override `log.fmt` directly: >lua
+    log.fmt = function(current_level, level, ...) ... end

comment_data.body:
> Makes sense, but defining a formatter is usually a create-time decision. Until there is a real use-case I don't think we need to care about this.

This might not be the only decision during creation. IIRC, our docs or discussion that mentioned `vim.notify` can be used directly as this `format_func`. This use case seems very useful and is likely the reason `set_format_func` exists.

> So in that rare case, what is the worst that might happen?

This could lead to the loss of the log entries that haven't been written to the buffer yet.

Besides, I think it's best to consider `format_func` alongside the support for different sinks we mentioned earlier. The `format_func` API likely already assumes that our logs are always printed to a file. If we support a sink that only accepts JSON data, the existence of `set_format_func` might become somewhat awkward.

comment_data.body:
> our docs or discussion that mentioned `vim.notify` can be used directly as this `format_func`. This use case seems very useful and is likely the reason `set_format_func` exists.

but why can't that be done at creation time?

hooking-into logs so that messages can fan-out to various sinks is definitely something we want to support. https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/35558 mentions adding a `Log` event (autocmd), that may be the way to add "sinks". `format_func` is [yet another](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19156) "event emitter" pattern...
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3882,14 +3882,15 @@ Example: >lua

 <
 
-You can also provide a custom formatter function to customize the log output
-format.
+To customize the output format, override `log.fmt` directly: >lua
+    log.fmt = function(current_level, level, ...) ... end

comment_data.body: Good point. We want composability but I think we can achieve that without inheritance.

comment_data.body:
> but why can't that be done at creation time?

@clason had the same question, and my explanation is here: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38906#discussion_r3063712336.

> hooking-into logs so that messages can fan-out to various sinks is definitely something we want to support. #35558 mentions adding a `Log` event (autocmd), that may be the way to add "sinks". `format_func` is [yet another](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19156) "event emitter" pattern...

I am also thinking about this; perhaps what we need at this moment is not a custom formatting feature, but rather a custom sinks feature. If that's the case, it might be better to keep `set_format_func` private until the sink functionality is implemented.

comment_data.body:
> yes, but I'm planning to drop `set_format_func` since I don't see a strong need for it meanwhile.

Oh, I only mentioned making it private because `set_format_func` is backed for a public API with the same name in `vim.lsp.log`. I'm fine with removing it, but we would use another implementation, maybe by overriding.
